steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The underlined claim is that Bianchini's calculations contain the earliest example of a decimal point. To support this, a finding must show an actual dot in Bianchini's work appearing in a numerical context that indicates a non-whole value — the defining feature of a decimal point.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Several dots appear in both Clavius's Astrolabium and Bianchini's Flores Almagesti, but the dot was commonly used in manuscripts in the 1400s and 1500s as a means to separate letters or whole numbers from each other.: This undermines the claim by suggesting the dots aren't decimal points at all. ✗
  • Choice B — In Clavius's Astrolabium, he gives several examples of both interpolation and inverse interpolation using decimal numbers and justifies the method to his readers by equating 45.7 with "457 tenths.": This is about Clavius, not Bianchini, and does nothing to establish Bianchini's priority. ✗
  • Choice C — In a table in Bianchini's astronomical Latin manuscript from the 1440s, Tabulae primi mobilis B, a dot appears between two numbers in a context that suggests the value is not a whole number.: This directly shows Bianchini using a dot as a decimal point in an astronomical table — exactly the evidence the claim requires. ✓
  • Choice D — As recorded in his manuscripts, some of Bianchini's calculations for the coordinates of the planets are inaccurate due to the use of whole numbers rather than non-whole-number decimals.: This shows Bianchini sometimes failed to use decimals, which weakens rather than supports the claim. ✗
That is the correct answer.
